modules.module: M1: Why Side Projects Die — Agile, Backlogs, Sprints, Feedback Loops

Claude.notes: Marcus's stalled internship-scanner story creates the exact problem each lesson resolves. Characters (Marcus, Priya, Jordan, Amara) are introduced cleanly with specific dates and reused throughout the course, establishing strong continuity. The Agile Manifesto, Scrum, Kanban, and LIRA framework are explained with clarity that matches the Advanced level. Quizzes consistently test interpretation rather than recall. Lab framing is excellent — the AI partner persona is opinionated by design.
Claude.topIssue: L1 occasionally hands the learner the insight directly rather than letting it land — e.g. 'The pattern is especially acute with AI-assisted projects because…' tells rather than shows.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of sprint, backlog, and velocity are technically sound. The Agile Manifesto attribution (2001, Utah, seventeen developers) is correct. Scrum and Kanban are distinguished accurately at the depth this audience needs. The Napster/Fanning anecdote is loose — Napster was released in June 1999 and the 'roughly nine months' framing oversimplifies a longer development arc. The lab is well-designed conceptually but its executability hinges on a connected LLM backend that is not described in the lesson.
Gemini.topIssue: The Napster/Fanning timeline ('shipped Napster in roughly nine months') is a simplification that a technically rigorous reader would push back on.

Perplexity.notes: Tool references — Lovable, Bolt.new, Cursor, Replit Agent, Claude — are all current within the last 12 months and correctly characterized as natural-language-to-app generators. The 2024–2025 framing of the AI side-project landscape matches what's actually happening in the indie-builder community. Eric Ries's three-validated-sprints rule is accurately attributed to 2011 lean startup methodology.
Perplexity.topIssue: The cited examples are current, but a learner reading this in late 2026 may want a contemporaneous example beyond the 2024 cohort.

modules.module: M2: Scoping Your Idea — Vagueness, Customer Specificity, MVP

Claude.notes: The Vagueness Detector lab and the Customer Specificity Ladder both translate the lesson directly into action. Reuse of Marcus and Priya alongside new characters (Theo, Devon, Jasmine) extends the canonical cast without overcrowding. The Five Whys framing is well-handled at this level.
Claude.topIssue: The 'Three Flavors of Vague' section telegraphs the answer before the story has finished posing the question.

Gemini.notes: MVP and First Transaction are described correctly. The Five Whys is presented as a scoping tool but its origin (Sakichi Toyoda / Toyota Production System) is not attributed, which weakens the case study fidelity. The 'Could I Call Them' test is operationally sound.
Gemini.topIssue: The Five Whys is introduced without attribution to its Toyota Production System origin — a notable omission for a technically rigorous read.

Perplexity.notes: The customer-specificity discussion holds up against current indie-maker discourse (Pieter Levels, Indie Hackers norms). The 'Everyone Trap' framing reflects how 2024–2025 builders actually fail. MVP framing is consistent with current best practice in micro-SaaS communities.
Perplexity.topIssue: The lab framework is current, but learners would benefit from a concrete 2025-era example product walked through end-to-end.

modules.module: M3: Sprint Cycles for Solo Builders — Vertical Slicing, Reviews, Pivots

Claude.notes: Vertical slicing is introduced through a story (Daniela's project) and then formalized — a clean problem-then-concept handoff. The Dependency Trap and 4-Question Scope Filter are concrete enough to apply immediately. Character cast continuity (Marcus, Priya, Jordan + new Daniela) is well-managed.
Claude.topIssue: The 10-Sprint Check-In is a coined construct — fine, but the lesson could acknowledge that explicitly so learners don't think it's standard Agile vocabulary.

Gemini.notes: Vertical slicing is described accurately as a technique for breaking features into deployable end-to-end units, consistent with how it's used in Scrum/XP literature. One-week vs. two-week sprint trade-offs are realistic. AI-Specific Scoping correctly identifies model behavior as a core uncertainty.
Gemini.topIssue: Vertical slicing is presented without attribution to its XP/Scrum origins, which a technical reader would expect.

Perplexity.notes: Solo-sprint adaptation is consistent with how indie builders actually work in 2025 — short cycles, real shipping cadence. Pivot-vs-distraction language reflects the current Lenny's Newsletter / Indie Hackers vocabulary. Living Roadmap framing is current and pragmatic.
Perplexity.topIssue: Pacing in the 'Graduating From Sprints' section is slightly thin — the current state of solo builder tooling could be better referenced.

modules.module: M4: AI Tools for Every Stage — Research, Build, Test, Iterate

Claude.notes: Tool-stack-by-stage framing is genuinely useful — research vs. build vs. test vs. iteration aren't the same job, and the lesson holds that line carefully. The Three-Question Filter before accepting any AI-suggested feature is one of the most operationally sharp practical takeaways in the whole course.
Claude.topIssue: The Aisha opening story is shorter and less fully drawn than Marcus's or Priya's — the narrative-density-match dips slightly.

Gemini.notes: Tool descriptions are accurate but the lesson treats specific products (Cursor, Lovable, Bolt.new, Replit Agent) as if they had stable, well-defined capability boundaries. They don't — these tools change capabilities monthly. A learner relying on this in six months may be misinformed about what each tool actually does.
Gemini.topIssue: The 'Right Build Stack by Project Type' section names specific tools without versioning or a 'check current capabilities' caveat — this content will date faster than the rest of the course.

Perplexity.notes: Tool currency is the structural risk in this module. As of mid-2026, several of the cited tools have shifted positioning (Replit Agent has been re-branded; Cursor's 'agent mode' has eclipsed the assist-only framing the lesson assumes). The Persist/Pivot/Quit framework is timeless but the tool-level guidance is the part most likely to feel dated.
Perplexity.topIssue: Replit Agent and Cursor have shifted product framing in 2026; the build-stack guidance needs a refresh to reflect current capability boundaries.

modules.module: M5: Handling Scope Creep When AI Makes Everything Feel Possible

Claude.notes: The 'Yes Machine Problem' framing is one of the most original concept anchors in the course — it names a real failure mode that emerges specifically because AI tools never refuse. Jordan and Priya recur as anchor characters; Darius and Maya add range. The MVB (Minimum Viable Boundary) framework is a clean inversion of MVP that earns its space.
Claude.topIssue: The Sunk Cost Trap section is shorter than its importance warrants — this is where most builders actually break.

Gemini.notes: The dopamine-loop framing is a reasonable popular-psychology summary. Sunk Cost is correctly characterized. The 'Three Types of Scope Creep AI Accelerates' is a useful taxonomy though not derived from a specific cited source.
Gemini.topIssue: The dopamine-loop framing simplifies neuroscience in a way that's effective rhetorically but a little loose — could benefit from a hedge.

Perplexity.notes: The 'AI as a yes machine' framing reflects exactly what 2025 indie builders complain about with Cursor/Claude/Lovable. The Parking Lot technique is a current PM staple. Triage Session framing matches how solo founders actually un-stick over-built projects in 2025–2026.
Perplexity.topIssue: The lab depends on AI partner backend wiring; without that, learners can't practice the Scope Audit interactively.

modules.module: M6: Getting Real Feedback Fast — Cold Outreach, Iteration, Pivots

Claude.notes: The 'Three Types of Positive Feedback That Should Worry You' is a sharp and useful taxonomy. The Iteration vs. Pivot distinction is handled honestly. The 'Emotional Side of Pivoting' acknowledgment is unusual for a curriculum module and lands well.
Claude.topIssue: Cold-outreach lab is structured but learners may struggle without sample scripts at the bottom of the section — the lab itself supplies them only via AI partner.

Gemini.notes: Validation taxonomy is reasonable. The 15-Minute Research Call structure is consistent with how Rob Fitzpatrick's Mom Test methodology is actually applied. The lesson does not formally cite Fitzpatrick here even though it leans on his approach — a missed attribution.
Gemini.topIssue: The 15-Minute Research Call template draws on Mom Test methodology without naming it — should attribute to Rob Fitzpatrick.

Perplexity.notes: Cold outreach guidance reflects 2025 reality — DM-first culture on X, niche Discord communities, founder LinkedIn outreach. The 'Where to Find Real Users Before You Have Any' section maps to platforms learners actually use today.
Perplexity.topIssue: The pivot/iterate distinction holds up but the lesson could reference at least one 2024–2025 named pivot story to anchor it in current builder reality.

modules.module: M7: Turning a Side Project into a Portfolio Piece or Income Stream

Claude.notes: Yusuf's documentation-failure opening is a useful new character arc. The 'Five Elements of a Hirable Project Presentation' is operationally sharp. The shift from product to portfolio framing is handled smoothly and the responsibility-curve framing for paying customers is honest about what charging actually changes.
Claude.topIssue: The narrative density dips here vs. earlier modules — the topic is more career/business so the protagonist's internal arc is thinner.

Gemini.notes: Pricing-psychology and AI cost-management sections are accurate but high-level. Stripe / Lemon Squeezy / Gumroad as 'tools that make charging possible in an afternoon' is correct as of 2026. Ethical considerations around small-scale AI use are presented sensibly without over-claiming.
Gemini.topIssue: Pricing psychology is summarized rather than sourced — anchoring, decoy effect, etc. could be attributed (Ariely / Kahneman).

Perplexity.notes: Platform choices reflect current portfolio reality (GitHub README + live demo + Loom walkthrough is the 2025 standard). AI cost guidance is genuinely useful and current — caching, prompt routing, smaller-model fallback are all live concerns now.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 'Three Paths Forward' closing is solid but the income-stream pricing examples could reference 2025 indie-hacker rev numbers to feel concrete.

modules.module: M8: Your First Sprint — 30-Day Plan, Daily Discipline, Retrospective

Claude.notes: This is the integrative module and it works. Lab 2 produces a real artifact — a 30-day sprint plan the learner walks away with. The 'Week 3 Wall', 'Recovery Protocol', and '85% Rule' all earn their place by being specific and operationalizable. Capability delta is unambiguous: 'After this lesson, this learner can run their own 30-day sprint to a launchable artifact.'
Claude.topIssue: The Daily Check-In Ritual could be condensed — section is slightly over-padded for an Advanced reader.

Gemini.notes: The 85% Rule is presented as a heuristic without source — it works as a practical constraint. The Sprint Viability Filter is internally consistent. Retrospective framing is faithful to standard Agile retrospective methodology.
Gemini.topIssue: The 85% Rule is presented as if established — clarifying it as a coined constraint specific to this course would improve technical honesty.

Perplexity.notes: 30-day sprint structure mirrors how indie builders are actually shipping in 2025–2026 (Pieter Levels' 12 startups in 12 months, Build in Public movement). 'Launch' definition is current — the bar is no longer 'feature complete' but 'one stranger paid or returned.'
Perplexity.topIssue: The launch definition is current as of 2026 but the 'What Comes Next' framing could reference current indie-builder community channels (X, Indie Hackers, Maker.so).
